title: DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transformer-based pre-trained language models have proven to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks. We argue that only taking single layer’s output restricts the power of pre-trained representation. Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer. Utilizing RoBERTa as the backbone encoder, our proposed improvement over the pre-trained models is shown effective on multiple natural language understanding tasks and help our model rival with the state-of-the-art models on the GLUE benchmark.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Language representation is essential to the understanding of text. Recently, pre-trained language models based on Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) such as GPT (Radford et al., 2018), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) have been shown to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. These models have since continued to achieve new state-of-the-art results on a variety of natural language processing tasks. They include question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2017), natural language inference (Williams et al., 2018; Bowman et al., 2015), named entity recognition (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003), sentiment analysis (Socher et al., 2013) and semantic textual similarity (Cer et al., 2017; Dolan & Brockett, 2005).

Normally, Transformer-based models are pre-trained on large-scale unlabeled corpus in an unsupervised manner, and then fine-tuned on the downstream tasks through introducing task-specific output layer. When fine-tuning on the supervised downstream tasks, the models pass directly the output of Transformer encoder’s final layer, which is considered as the contextualized representation of input text, to the task-specific layer.

However, due to the numerous layers (i.e., Transformer blocks) and considerable depth of these pretrained models, we argue that the output of the last layer may not always be the best representation of the input text during the fine-tuning for downstream tasks. Devlin et al. (2019) shows diverse combinations of different layers’ outputs of the pre-trained BERT result in distinct performance on CoNLL-2003 Named Entity Recognition (NER) task (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Peters et al. (2018b) points out for pre-trained language models, including Transformer, the most transferable contextualized representations of input text tend to occur in the middle layers, while the top layers specialize for language modeling. Therefore, the onefold use of the last layer’s output may restrict the power of the pre-trained representation.

In this paper, we propose an extra network component design for Transformer-based model, which is capable of adaptively leveraging the hidden information in the Transformer’s hidden layers to refine the language representation. Our introduced additional components include two main additional components:
1. HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

2. Fusion network integrates the hidden information extracted by the HIRE with Transformer final layer’s output through two steps of functionalities, leading to a refined contextualized language representation.

Taking advantage of the robustness of RoBERTa by using it as our backbone Transformer-based encoder (Liu et al., 2019c), we conduct experiments on GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2018), which consists of nine Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks. With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset. Keeping the backbone Transformer model unchanged on its architecture, pre-training procedure and training objectives, we get comparable performance with other state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed HIRE enhancement over Transformer model.

## 2.1 TRANSFORMER-BASED ENCODER

Transformer-based encoder is responsible for encoding input text into contextualized representation. Let _{w_ 1 _, . . . , wn}_ represent a sequence of _n_ words of input text, Transformer-based encoder encodes the input sequence into its universal contextualized representation _**R** ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ :

where _d_ is the hidden size of the encoder and _**R**_ is the output of Transformer-based encoder’s last layer which has the same length as the input text. We call it **preliminary representation** in this paper to distinguish it with the one that we introduce in section 2.2. Here, we omit a rather extensive formulations of Transformer and refer readers to Vaswani et al. (2017), Radford et al. (2018) and Devlin et al. (2019) for more details.

## 2.2 HIDDEN REPRESENTATION EXTRACTOR

Transformer-based encoder normally has many structure-identical layers stacked together, for example, BERTLARGE and XLNetLARGE all contain 24 layers of the identical structure, either outputs

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

from these hidden layers or the last layer, but not only limited to the latter, may be extremely helpful for specific downstream task. To make full use of the representations from these hidden layers, we introduce an extra component attached to the original encoder, HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) to capture the complementary information that the output of the last layer fails to capture. Since each layer does not take the same importance to represent a certain input sequence for different downstream tasks, we design an adaptive mechanism that can compute the importance dynamically. We measure the importance by an **importance score** .

The input to the HIRE is _{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}_ where _l_ represents the number of layers in the encoder. Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ . For the sake of simplicity, we call them all hidden-state afterwards.

We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. In this way, we manage to summarize the hidden-state into a fixed-sized vector. Hence, we obtain _**U** ∈_ R[(] _[l]_[+1)] _[×]_[4] _[d]_ with _**u** i_ the summarized vector of _**H** i_ :

where 0 _≤ i ≤ l_ . Then the importance value _αi_ for hidden-state _**H** i_ is calculated by:

where _**W** ∈_ R[4] _[d][×]_[1] and _b ∈_ R are trainable parameters. Let _**α**_ = _{αi}_ be normalized into a probability distribution _**s**_ through a softmax layer:
where _si_ is the normalized weight of hidden-state _i_ when computing the representation. Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence’s new representation _**A**_ by:

With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .

## 2.3 FUSION NETWORK

This module fuses the information contained in the output of Transformer-based encoder and the one extracted from encoders’ hidden states by HIRE.

Given the preliminary representation _**R**_ , instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer, we combine it together with the complementary representation _**A**_ to yield _**M**_ , defined by:
where _◦_ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.

Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:

## 2.4 OUTPUT LAYER

The output layer is task-specific. The following are the concerned implementation details on two tasks, classification and regression.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

For classification task, given the input text’s contextualized representation _**F**_ , following Devlin et al. (2019), we take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation. Let _m_ be the number of labels in the datasets, we pass _**c**_ through a feed-forward network (FFN):

with _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R[2] _[d][×][d]_ , _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][×][m]_ , _**b**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d]_ and _**b**_ 2 _∈_ R _[m]_ the only parameters that we introduce in output layer. Finally, the probability distribution of predicted label is computed as:
For regression task, we obtain _q_ in the same manner with _m_ = 1, and take _q_ as the predicted value.

## 2.5 TRAINING

For classification task, the training loss to be minimized is defined by the Cross-Entropy:
where _**θ**_ is the set of all parameters in the model, _T_ is the number of examples in the dataset, _pi,c_ is the predicted probability of gold class _c_ for example _i_ .

For regression task, we define the training loss by mean squared error (MSE):

where _qi_ is the predicted value for example _i_ and _yi_ is the ground truth value for example _i_ .

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET

We conducted the experiments on the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark (Wang et al., 2018) to evaluate our proposed method. GLUE is a collection of 9 diverse datasets[1] for training, evaluating, and analyzing natural language understanding models.

Table 1: GLUE Dev results. Our results are based on single model trained with single task and a median over five runs with different random seed but the same hyperparameter is reported for each task. The results of MT-DNN, XLNETLARGE, ALBERT and RoBERTa are from Liu et al. (2019b), Yang et al. (2019), Lan et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2019c). See the lower-most row for the performance of our approach. Mc, acc and pearson denote Matthews correlation, accuracy and Person correlation coefficient respectively.

> 1All the datasets can be obtained from https://gluebenchmark.com/tasks

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

|Model|Params|Shared|
|---|---|---|
||(M)|(M)|
|ALBERT-xxlarge|235|-|
|MT-DNN|350|340|
|RoBERTa|355|-|
|RoBERTa+HIRE|437|-|

Table 2: Parameter Comparison.

Table 1 compares our method with a list of Transformer-based models on the development set. Model parameter comparison is shown in Table 2. To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa, following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data. The single-model results for each task are reported. We run our model with five different random seeds but the same hyperparameters and take the median value. Due to the problematic nature of WNLI dataset, we exclude its results in this table. The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets, with an improvement of 1.7 points, 0.4 points, 0.5/0.2 points, 0.3 points on CoLA, SST-2, MNLI and QNLI respectively. And on the MRPC, STS-B and RTE task, our model get the same result as RoBERTa. It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.

Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard). The number below each task’s name indicates the size of training dataset. Recently the GLUE benchmark has forbidden all the submissions to treat the QNLI as a ranking task, which results in the missing of some models’ accuracies on the QNLI. _[a]_ Devlin et al. (2019); _[b]_ Liu et al. (2019b); _[c]_ Zhu et al. (2020); _[d]_ Yang et al. (2019); _[e]_ Lan et al. (2020); _[f]_ Liu et al. (2019c).

Table 3 presents the results of HIRE enhancement and other models on the test set that have been submitted to the GLUE leaderboard. Following Liu et al. (2019c), we fine-tune STS-B and MRPC starting from the MNLI single-task model. Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI, and the large-scale nature of MNLI dataset (393k), we also initialize RoBERTa+HIRE with the weights of MNLI single-task model before fine-tuning on RTE and WNLI. We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set. To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI with an improvement of 0.8 points, 0.4 points, 0.7/0.9 points, 0.2/0.1 points, 0.2 points and 0.1 points respectively. In the meantime, RoBERTa+HIRE gets the same results as RoBERTa on QQP and WNLI. By category, RoBERTa+HIRE has better performance than RoBERTa on the single sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks. It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1. The results are quite promising since HIRE does not modify the encoder internal architecture (Yang et al., 2019) or redefine the pre-training procedure (Liu et al., 2019c) , getting the comparable results with them.

## 4 ABLATION STUDY

In this section, we perform a set of ablation experiments to understand the effects of our proposed techniques during fine-tuning. All the results reported in this section are a median of five random runs.

## 4.1 MODEL DESIGN CONSIDERATION

||CoLA|STS-B|
|---|---|---|
|Model|(Mc)|(Pearson)|
|Our model|69.7|92.4|
|w/o HIRE|68.5|91.5|
|w/o Fusion network|68.2|92.2|

Table 4: Ablation study over model design consideration on the development set of CoLA and STSB. The result for each model is a median of five random runs. Both HIRE and fusion network significantly improve the model performance on all two datasets.

To individually evaluate the importance of HIRE and fusion network, we vary our model in the following ways and conduct the experiments on the development set of CoLA and STS-B. The results are in Table 4:

- We remove the HIRE from our model and pass the preliminary representation _**R**_ directly into the fusion network. In order to keep the fusion network, we fuse the preliminary representation with itself, which means we define instead _**M**_ by:

The results are presented in row 2.

- We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer and present the results in row 3.

As can be seen from the table, to use of HIRE to extract the hidden information is crucial to the model: Matthews correlation of CoLA and Pearson correlation coefficient of STS-B drop dramatically by 1.2 and 0.9 points if it’s removed. We also observe that fusion network is an important component that contributes 1.5/0.2 gains on the CoLA and STS-B tasks. The results are aligned with our assumption that HIRE extracts the complementary information from the hidden layers while the fusion network fuses it with the preliminary representation.

We investigate how the adaptive assignment mechanism of the importance score affects the model’s performance. CoLA is chosen as the downstream task. Table 5 compares our proposed mechanism

|Number|0|1|2|3|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Mc|68.5|69.3|**69.7**|68.5|
Table 6: Ablation study over GRU layer number in fusion network on the development set of CoLA. The best result is in **bold** .

with diverse fixed combinations of importance scores over different range of layers: first 6, last 6 or all 25 layers. Two strategies are studied: _mean_ and _random_ . In the _mean_ situation, we suppose all the layers contribute exactly the same. On the contrary, under the _random_ condition, we generate a score for each layer randomly and do the SoftMax operation across all the layers.

From the table, we observe that fixing each layer’s importance score for all examples hurts the model performance. Across all rows, we find that none of single strategy can yield the best performance. The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.

## 4.3 EFFECT OF GRU LAYER NUMBER IN FUSION NETWORK

To investigate the effect of GRU layer number in the fusion network, we set the GRU layer number from 0 to 3 and conduct the ablation experiments on the development dataset of CoLA. Table 6 shows the results. We observe that the modest number 2 would be a better choice.

## 5 ANALYSIS

Figure 2: **Left** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers when computing the complementary representation for various NLU tasks. The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer. **Right** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example.

We compare the importance score’s distribution of different NLU tasks. For each task, we run our best single model over the development set and the results are calculated by averaging the values across all the examples within each dataset. The results are presented in the left of Figure 2. From the top to the bottom of the heatmap, the results are placed in the following order: single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and natural language inference tasks. From it, we find that the distribution differs among the different tasks, which demonstrates HIRE’s dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation. The most important contribution occurs below the final layer for all the tasks except STS-B and RTE. All layers have a close contribution for STS-B and RTE task. In addition, we find that QNLI, a classification task which requires the model to determine whether the context sentence contains the answer to the question, relies on layers 2-4 to some extent. In fact, Jawahar et al. (2019) state that BERT mostly captures phrase-level (or span-level) information in the lower layers and that this information gets gradually diluted in higher layers. Since QNLI is derived from the span extraction question answering dataset

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

SQuAD, where the answer is a span of the input text, we speculate that HIRE takes advantage of this information when making the prediction.

The right of Figure 2 presents the distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example. It shows that HIRE can adapt not only distinct tasks but also the different examples. In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them. But the figure shows also that for some examples, all layers contribute nearly equally.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Transformer model is empowered by self-attention mechanism and has been applied as an effective model architecture design in quite a lot of pre-trained language models (Vaswani et al., 2017). OpenAI GPT (Radford et al., 2018) is the first model that introduced Transformer architecture into unsupervised pre-training. But instead of unidirectional training like GPT, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) adopts Masked LM objective when pre-training, which enables the representation to incorporate context from both direction. The next sentence prediction (NSP) objective is also used by BERT to better model the relationship between sentences. Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories. In terms of finetuning on downstream tasks, all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results. Diverse new methods have been proposed recently for fine-tuning the downstream tasks, including multi-task learning (Liu et al., 2019b), adversarial training (Zhu et al., 2020) or incorporating semantic information into language representation (Zhang et al., 2020).

Traditionally, downstream tasks or back-end part of models take representations from the last layer of the pre-trained language models as the default input. However, recent studies trigger researchers’ interest on the intermediate layers’ representation learned by pre-trained models. When studying the linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized word representations with a series of seventeen diverse probing tasks, Liu et al. (2019a) observe that Transformers tend to encode transferable features in their intermediate layers, aligned with the results by Peters et al. (2018b). Jawahar et al. (2019) show a rich hierarchy of linguistic information is encoded by BERT, with surface features, syntactic features and semantic features lying from bottom to top in the layers. So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models. However, we want to note that a freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist, since previous observations are made by adopting a feature extracting approach while our work is conducted in the fine-tuning scenario.

Several works have made attempts to utilize the information from the intermediate layers of deep models (Zhao et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019). Peters et al. (2018a) propose a deep contextualized word representation called ELMo, which is a task-specific linear combination of the intermediate layer representations in the bidirectional LSTM language model. Tenney et al. (2019) find that using ELMo-style scalar mixing of layer activations, both deep Transformer models (BERT and GPT) gain a significant performance improvement on a novel edge probing task. Zhu et al. (2018) adopt a linear combination of embeddings from different layers in BERT to encode tokens for conversational question answering but in a feature-based way. Compared with these works, our approach still presents the following innovations: 1) Our dynamic layer weighting mechanism calculates importance scores for each example over different layers, which is distinct from previous works (e.g., ELMo), where the contribution score (in the form of a parameter) for each layer is always same for all the examples of the same task. 2) Our approach fuses the representation weighted from the hidden layers and the one from the last layer to get a refined representation. 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 7 CONCLUSION

This paper presents HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), a novel enhancement component that refines language representation by adaptively leveraging the Transformer-based model’s hidden layers. In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer. A lite fusion network then incorporates the outputs of HIRE into those of the original model. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of refined language representation for natural language understanding. The analysis highlights the distinct contribution of each layer’s output for diverse tasks and different examples.

- Pranav Rajpurkar, Robin Jia, and Percy Liang. Know what you don’t know: Unanswerable questions for SQuAD. In _Proceedings of the 56th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL) (Volume 2: Short Papers)_ , pp. 784–789, July 2018.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a sensible architectural enhancement for fine-tuning pre-trained Transformers by adaptively weighting hidden layer representations. The motivation is well-grounded in existing literature regarding the layer-wise distribution of linguistic features. However, the technical execution contains several significant notation inconsistencies and mathematical ambiguities, particularly regarding the dimensionality of tensors and the exact operation for combining token-level versus sequence-level representations. The transition from token-level hidden states to a fixed-size vector for weighting, and then back to a sequence-level complementary representation, lacks sufficient formal clarity and contains a likely dimensionality error in the fusion network description.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated approach to enhancing pre-trained language models by extracting and fusing information from intermediate transformer layers via a dynamic mechanism (HIRE) and a fusion network. The architecture is clearly described, and the empirical evaluation on GLUE is thorough, including ablation studies and analysis of learned importance scores. The method shows consistent, albeit modest, improvements over RoBERTa, particularly on classification tasks. The core ideas are sound and align with known insights about layer specialization in Transformers. However, there are several technical inconsistencies in the mathematical formulation and notation that affect clarity and reproducibility. These include mismatched dimensions, ambiguous definitions, and skipped justification for key design choices, which slightly undermine confidence in the implementation details. Despite these issues, the overall contribution is solid and the conclusions are largely supported by the results.

comments.title: Incorrect dimensionality in HIRE output concatenation
comments.quote: we obtain **U** ∈ R[(] [l][+1)] [×][4] [d] with **u** i the summarized vector of **H** i
comments.explanation: The text states that **U** ∈ ℝ^[(l+1)×4d] where each **u**_i is a summarized vector from the Bi-GRU. However, a 2-layer Bi-GRU produces 4 hidden states per timestep (forward and backward for each of two layers). The final state vector per layer is typically of size d, so concatenating them yields a 4d-dimensional vector. This suggests that **u**_i ∈ ℝ^{4d}, and thus **U** should be a sequence of l+1 such vectors, i.e., **U** ∈ ℝ^{(l+1)×4d}, which matches the stated dimension. However, the subsequent computation of α_i uses a linear projection **W** ∈ ℝ^{4d×1}, applied to each **u**_i, which implies α_i is scalar per layer — this part is consistent. The issue arises in the next step: equation for **A** = Σ_i s_i **H**_i, where **H**_i ∈ ℝ^{n×d}, so **A** ∈ ℝ^{n×d}. But **H**_i is not directly related to **u**_i, which was used to compute weights. The model appears to use **u**_i only to compute s_i, then applies s_i to the original hidden states **H**_i. This is logically sound, but the exposition confounds summarization of **H**_i into **u**_i with the weighted combination of **H**_i — a reader might initially think **A** is built from **u**_i, but it is not. The text should clarify that **u**_i is used only for scoring, not representation. While not a mathematical error per se, the dimensional mapping is misleadingly presented.

comments.title: Ambiguity in fusion network input dimension and operation
comments.explanation: In the ablation study (Table 4), the authors define a variant where preliminary representation _**R**_ is fused with itself: _**M**_ = [_**R**_; _**R**_; _**R**_ + _**R**_; _**R** ◦ _**R**_]. This leads to a concatenated tensor of shape n×4d. But _**R**_ appears four times: twice as-is, once as 2_**R**_, and once as _**R** ◦ _**R**_. This raises concerns: (1) Why duplicate _**R**_ explicitly? (2) The element-wise square _**R** ◦ _**R**_ and scaled version 2_**R**_ are nonlinear transformations, but they are concatenated alongside raw _**R**_, which may bias the GRU toward the duplicated raw signal. More critically, in the main model (Section 2.3), _**M**_ = [_**R**; **A**; **R** + **A**; **R** ◦ **A**], which implies symmetry between **R** and **A**, yet in the ablation, when **A** is absent, they do not use [_**R**; **R**; **R**+**R**; **R**◦**R**] as an asymmetric substitute — this is inconsistent. The real issue is that in the ablation, they replace **A** with **R**, which makes _**M**_ = 4 copies of _**R**_ in different forms, whereas in the real model, **A** and **R** are distinct. Thus, the 'w/o HIRE' baseline is not a clean ablation — it introduces a different inductive bias. A better baseline would be to set **A**=0 or **A**=**R** only once. This undermines the interpretability of the ablation result.

comments.title: Missing normalization in HIRE importance score derivation
comments.quote: Let _**α**_ = _{αi}_ be normalized into a probability distribution _**s**_ through a softmax layer
comments.explanation: The text states that α_i is computed via a linear projection (**W** ∈ ℝ^{4d×1}) and bias, then normalized via softmax to produce s_i. However, the dimension of **u**_i is 4d and **W** is 4d×1, so α_i is scalar — correct. But the softmax is applied over i=0 to l, so s_i = exp(α_i)/Σ_j exp(α_j). This is standard. However, the model includes **H**_0, the initial embedding, in the set of hidden states to be weighted. This is unusual because **H**_0 is not a transformer layer output but an input embedding. Including it is not invalid, but the paper does not justify why it should be treated the same. More importantly, when applying softmax over l+1 elements (layers 0 through l), the weighting includes this uncontextualized embedding on equal footing with deep contextualized layers. This could dilute meaningful signals. The ablation in Table 5 refers to 'all 25 layers', but RoBERTa-large has 24 transformer layers plus input — so '25' includes **H**_0. This is correct per their definition, but non-standard. The paper should clarify whether **H**_0 is updated during training (it is, per Sec 2.2) — but still, its inclusion in the softmax pool may be questionable. However, since it's consistently defined, it's a design choice rather than an error.

comments.title: Inconsistency between GRU usage in HIRE vs. Fusion Network
comments.quote: we use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)... to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder
comments.explanation: The paper states that a 2-layer Bi-GRU is used to summarize each **H**_j (a matrix of size n×d) into a fixed vector **u**_j. However, a GRU applied to **H**_j would process the sequence of n tokens. The final hidden states (forward and backward for each layer) are then concatenated to form **u**_j ∈ ℝ^{4d}. This is standard. Later, in the fusion network, another 2-layer Bi-GRU is applied to **M** ∈ ℝ^{n×4d} to produce **F**. But here, the input dimension is 4d, while the HIRE GRUs processed inputs of dimension d. The paper does not specify whether the GRU parameters are shared between HIRE and fusion network — but it says 'the same 2-layer Bi-GRU' in HIRE, and later 'two-layer bidirectional GRU' in fusion, without clarifying parameter sharing. The caption of Figure 1 says 'Bi-GRUs share the same parameters for each layer’s output', which likely refers to HIRE processing each **H**_j with tied weights. But does the fusion network use different weights? The text does not say. If they share parameters, that would be unusual given the different input dimensions. If not, it should be stated. This underspecification affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about state-of-the-art on SST-2
comments.quote: our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1
comments.explanation: The paper claims SOTA on SST-2 with 97.1 accuracy. However, Table 3 shows that both XLNet-large and ALBERT-xxlarge also achieve 97.1, tying with the proposed model. Therefore, it is not strictly 'state-of-the-art' in the sense of surpassing all prior models. The claim overstates the result. Even if the score is median over runs, the paper presents it as a single test score (from leaderboard), like others. The phrase 'state-of-the-art' implies superiority, not parity. A more accurate statement would be 'achieves state-of-the-art performance', acknowledging the tie. Given that multiple models achieve this score, the claim is slightly overstated.

comments.title: Parameter count inconsistency with model architecture
comments.quote: RoBERTa+HIRE|437|-
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports RoBERTa at 355M parameters and RoBERTa+HIRE at 437M, an increase of 82M. The added components are: (1) HIRE with a 2-layer Bi-GRU applied to each of 25 hidden states (but shared weights, so only one instance), and (2) a fusion network with another 2-layer Bi-GRU, (3) the scoring layer (4d×1 + bias), and (4) the output layer (which already existed in RoBERTa). The HIRE Bi-GRU: for input size d (typically 1024 for RoBERTa-large), hidden size presumably d, a 2-layer Bi-GRU has approximately 6×d×d parameters per layer (for gates and candidate), so 2×6×d² = 12×d². For d=1024, this is ~12.5M. The fusion GRU: input dimension 4d, output dimension d per direction, so parameters ≈ 2×6×(4d)×d = 48×d² ≈ 50M. Scoring layer: 4d×1 = ~4K. The rest negligible. Total added: ~62.5M, far less than 82M. Either the hidden size of the GRUs is larger, or there are additional components. The paper does not specify the GRU hidden sizes, making it impossible to verify. This constitutes underspecification — a key hyperparameter (GRU dimension) is omitted.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents HIRE, a novel approach to leverage intermediate layer representations in transformer models. The core idea is sound and well-motivated, with experiments showing consistent improvements over RoBERTa baseline on multiple GLUE tasks. However, there are several technical issues that need clarification, particularly around the mathematical formulation and architectural details. The paper would benefit from clearer notation, more complete equation derivations, and better specification of the model components and training procedure.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in HIRE input description
comments.quote: The input to the HIRE is _{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}_ where _l_ represents the number of layers in the encoder.
comments.explanation: The notation is inconsistent here - it uses both `_**H**_ j` (with subscript j) and `_**H** l` (with subscript l) when referring to layer outputs. This should be consistent throughout, likely using `_**H**_ j` for 0 ≤ j ≤ l.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of H₀ update mechanism
comments.quote: Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder's first layer but is updated during training
comments.explanation: This statement is confusing. If H₀ is the initial embedding that serves as input to the first encoder layer, how exactly is it 'updated during training'? Is it referring to gradient updates to the embedding layer, or is there some other mechanism? This needs clarification as it affects the understanding of what representations HIRE has access to.

comments.title: Dimensional mismatch in fusion network input
comments.quote: Given the preliminary representation _**R**_ , instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer, we combine it together with the complementary representation _**A**_ to yield _**M**_ , defined by: _**M**_ = [ _**R**_ ; _**A**_ ; _**R**_ + _**A**_ ; _**R**_ ◦ _**A**_ ] _∈_ R _[n][×]_[4] _[d]_
comments.explanation: The fusion concatenates R and A (both of shape [n×d]) along with their sum and element-wise product, resulting in shape [n×4d]. However, the subsequent two-layer bidirectional GRU is described as having 'output size of d for each direction'. It's unclear how the 4d-dimensional input M is processed by a BiGRU with d-sized hidden states. Does it first project down to d dimensions? This architectural detail is missing.

comments.title: Non-standard notation for bidirectional output
comments.quote: we take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation.
comments.explanation: The notation R[2] _[d]_ is non-standard and confusing. For a bidirectional GRU output with d dimensions per direction, the concatenated output should typically be denoted as R^(2d) or R^{2d}, not R[2][d]. While the meaning can be inferred, this notation is unconventional and could confuse readers.

comments.title: Missing training procedure details
comments.quote: The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't specify whether the backbone RoBERTa parameters are frozen or fine-tuned along with the HIRE components during training. This is crucial for understanding the nature of the improvement - is HIRE extracting features from a frozen encoder, or is the entire model being jointly optimized? Without this detail, the results are difficult to interpret and reproduce.

comments.title: Questionable ablation baseline
comments.quote: We remove the HIRE from our model and pass the preliminary representation _**R**_ directly into the fusion network. In order to keep the fusion network, we fuse the preliminary representation with itself, which means we define instead _**M**_ by: _**M**_ = [ _**R**_ ; _**R**_ ; _**R**_ + _**R**_ ; _**R**_ ◦_ _**R**_ ]
comments.explanation: This ablation study design is questionable. When removing HIRE, fusing R with itself (creating redundant features) is an artificial baseline that doesn't represent a realistic model architecture. A more meaningful baseline would be to remove the fusion network entirely and use R directly, or to replace A with a learned vector. The current design may exaggerate the importance of HIRE.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for GRU architecture
comments.quote: We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder.
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't justify why a 2-layer BiGRU is chosen for summarizing each layer's output, nor does it specify the hidden dimensions or other architectural details. Given that the hidden states H_j are already contextualized representations from transformers, it's unclear why an RNN is needed for summarization and why this specific architecture was chosen.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated approach to improving Transformer-based representations by dynamically aggregating hidden-layer information through an "HIRE" mechanism. The experimental results show modest but consistent gains on the GLUE benchmark. The technical description reaches a sufficient level of detail for reproduction, though there is a minor inconsistency in notation regarding the dimensions of the importance weights in the HIRE module.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces HIRE, a novel component designed to adaptively weight and fuse intermediate hidden states from pre-trained transformers with the final layer's output. The core idea is compelling and the experimental results on GLUE support the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, the manuscript suffers from several critical OCR-related omissions of mathematical formulas, underspecified details in the ablation study regarding dimensionality, and minor terminology/claim inconsistencies. Addressing these gaps, particularly by restoring the missing equations and clarifying the ablation setup, would significantly improve the paper's rigor and reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Formulas (OCR Artifacts)
comments.quote: encodes the input sequence into its universal contextualized representation _**R** ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ :
comments.explanation: Due to PDF extraction, key equations are missing throughout Sections 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4, and 2.5. While the surrounding prose allows reasonable inference (e.g., the complementary representation A is an attention-weighted sum of hidden states, and the fusion module M concatenates [R; A; R+A; R◦A]), the absence of formal definitions for the GRU summarization, attention computation, fusion network transformation, and loss functions prevents precise verification. This omission significantly hampers reproducibility and makes it difficult to confirm normalization steps or activation choices.

comments.title: Underspecified Dimension Handling in Ablation Study
comments.quote: We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer and present the results in row 3.
comments.explanation: The fusion network outputs a contextualized representation F of shape n x 2d, which feeds into the output layer (Section 2.4). When the fusion network is removed in the ablation, HIRE's output A retains shape n x d. The paper does not specify how the dimensionality mismatch is resolved: whether the authors project A to 2d, resize the first-layer weight matrix W_1, or use a different aggregation strategy. This ambiguity makes the ablation setup difficult to reproduce accurately.

comments.title: Terminology Inconsistency Regarding Layer Count
comments.quote: mean, all 25 layers
comments.explanation: Table 5 and Section 4.2 refer to 'all 25 layers', whereas the standard RoBERTa-large architecture consists of 24 transformer blocks. Given Section 2.2's inclusion of H_0 (the initial embedding) alongside H_1 to H_24, the count of 25 actually refers to 25 hidden states/outputs. The authors should clarify this terminology to distinguish between transformer blocks and extracted hidden states, avoiding confusion for readers.

comments.title: Overclaim in Related Work
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: While the dynamic, example-wise weighting mechanism is indeed novel, the claim of being the 'first' to leverage intermediate representations during fine-tuning is likely too strong. Prior literature has explored layer fusion, linear mixing, and attention over intermediate layers in fine-tuning settings. The authors should refine this claim to emphasize the novelty of their adaptive, input-specific weighting scheme rather than the general utilization of intermediate representations.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Model Definition and Ablation Study Formulas
comments.quote:
Section 2.3: \[M = [R; R \oplus A]\]

Section 4.1: \[M = [R; R; R + R; R \circ R]\]
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the fusion step is defined as \(M = [R; R \oplus A]\). The symbol \(\oplus\) is never explicitly defined (it could denote addition or a Hadamard product), but the operation is clearly an element-wise combination of \(R\) and \(A\). However, in Section 4.1's ablation study, the equation describing the setup without HIRE is \(M = [R; R; R + R; R \circ R]\). This formula describes a concatenation of four distinct vectors, which is radically different from the single concatenation described in Section 2.3. This inconsistency makes the ablation study mathematically incomprehensible and suggests a potential error in the transcription or the manuscript itself, as the row labeled 'w/o HIRE' suggests removing \(A\), yet the resulting formula does not simply replace \(A\) with \(R\).

comments.title: Ambiguity in Bi-GRU Implementation Strategy
comments.quote: Section 2.2: 'We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder.'
comments.explanation: The text implies that the Bi-GRU is applied to each hidden state \(H_i\) individually (e.g., \(BiGRU(H_0)\), \(BiGRU(H_1)\), ..., \(BiGRU(H_l)\)), suggesting independent processing with shared parameters or \((l+1)\) copies of the GRU. However, Table 2 shows that 'RoBERTa+HIRE' adds approximately 82 million parameters compared to the 355 million of RoBERTa-large. If the Bi-GRU processes each of the 25 hidden states (including embedding) individually, the parameter count would be significantly higher due to the \(N\times P\) scaling. The parameter count matches a model where a *single* shared Bi-GRU processes the sequence of hidden states \(\{H_0, ..., H_l\}\). While the numerical evidence confirms the latter is the intended implementation, the phrasing 'summarize each hidden-state' is technically misleading and could confuse readers attempting to reproduce the results.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency in GLUE Test Results Table
comments.quote: Table 3: RoBERTa row: 'Score' column value is '88.1', 'RTE' column value is '88.2'.
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the 'Score' column appears to represent a specific metric (possibly a composite score or leader board rank) for each model. However, for the RoBERTa baseline, the 'Score' column lists 88.1, while the specific 'RTE' task column lists 88.2. These values differ, yet for other baselines (like XLNet and RoBERTa+HIRE), the values seem to match the corresponding RTE columns exactly. This creates a discrepancy where the 'Score' label does not seem to correspond to the 'RTE' score, which would be highly unusual for a tabular comparison of this nature. The values should be consistent if 'Score' refers to the aggregate performance or a specific leaderboard metric for that entry.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a reasonable method for leveraging hidden layer representations from pre-trained Transformers via a learned weighting and fusion mechanism. The experimental results show moderate improvements on GLUE, and the ablation studies confirm the importance of both the HIRE module and the fusion network. However, the paper suffers from several exposition gaps: key equations are missing at critical points (the importance score computation and the feed-forward layer), the ablation setup when removing the fusion network is underspecified regarding output dimensions, and the claim of state-of-the-art on SST-2 is slightly overstated given tied scores. These issues, while not invalidating the core contribution, reduce reproducibility and clarity.

comments.title: Missing equation for importance score α_i
comments.quote: Then the importance value α_i for hidden-state H_i is calculated by: where W ∈ R^{4d×1} and b ∈ R are trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The text states that an importance value is calculated and that W and b are involved, but the actual formula (presumably a linear transformation of the summarized vector u_i) is omitted. While a reader familiar with attention mechanisms can infer the intended operation (e.g., α_i = u_i·W + b), the missing equation is a gap in the specification and could lead to confusion, especially for less-experienced readers.

comments.title: Missing equation for feed-forward network in output layer
comments.quote: we pass c through a feed-forward network (FFN): with W_1 ∈ R^{2d×d}, W_2 ∈ R^{d×m}, b_1 ∈ R^d and b_2 ∈ R^m the only parameters that we introduce in output layer. Finally, the probability distribution of predicted label is computed as:
comments.explanation: The FFN computation (likely a linear layer with GELU activation followed by another linear layer and softmax) is not written explicitly. The text only lists the weight dimensions and then jumps to the final distribution. This omission makes the exact output transformation ambiguous.

comments.title: Underspecified ablation when removing the fusion network
comments.quote: We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer and present the results in row 3.
comments.explanation: The output layer is designed to accept a representation of size 2d (from the Bi-GRU in the fusion network). HIRE’s output A has size d. Therefore, removing the fusion network would require modifying the output layer (e.g., changing the input dimension of the FFN from 2d to d). The text does not mention any such adaptation, making it unclear how the ablation was actually implemented. This could affect the validity of the comparison and is a reproducibility concern.

comments.title: Underspecified ablation on GRU layer number in fusion network
comments.quote: To investigate the effect of GRU layer number in the fusion network, we set the GRU layer number from 0 to 3 and conduct the ablation experiments on the development dataset of CoLA.
comments.explanation: The configuration with 0 GRU layers is ambiguous: if the GRU is removed entirely, the input to the output layer would be the M tensor of size 4d rather than the 2d produced by the Bi-GRU. The paper does not specify how the output layer is adjusted for this case, making the result (Mc=68.5) difficult to interpret.

comments.title: Overstatement of state-of-the-art on SST-2
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: In Table 3, ALBERT-xxlarge and XLNet-large also achieve 97.1 on SST-2. Therefore, the result is tied, not a unique state-of-the-art. The claim is slightly overstated and should be qualified (e.g., 'ties with' or 'matches' the state-of-the-art).

comments.title: Inclusion of final layer in complementary representation contradicts stated goal
comments.quote: HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.
comments.explanation: The HIRE computes A as a weighted sum over all hidden states including the final layer (H_0 through H_l). Since the final layer's output R is one of these states, A may contain information already present in R, rather than being strictly complementary. While the learned weights could mitigate this overlap, the name and description suggest a purer 'complementary' role. This is a minor conceptual inconsistency.
